subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear and appropriate purpose and focus throughout.
Research question is clearly stated and easily identifiable.
Use of peer-reviewed journal articles and landmark studies shows informed selection.
weaknesses: Research methodology is only partially documented; search strategy and inclusion/exclusion criteria lack transparency.
Scope of the research question (âto what extentâ) remains broad and occasionally drifts into background narrative.
Some background narrative (e.g., McMartin case) veers into detailed description rather than analytical focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (topic communication and research question formulation) align with the 3â4 band, while methodology sits in the 3â4 band at the upper end. Averaging these performances and considering clear strengths in communication but limitations in methodological detail yields a balanced score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Solid integration of developmental neuroscience and cognitive factors to support claims.
Accurate and mostly consistent use of subject-specific terminology and concepts (e.g., episodic memory, NICHD protocol).
Clear definitions of key constructs and effective use of real-world data to underscore significance.
weaknesses: Occasional factual imprecision (e.g., oversimplified brain maturation age) and minor conceptual lapses.
Heavy reliance on dated studies with limited incorporation of recent research developments.
Some operational terms (such as âreliabilityâ) remain vague or under-defined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands demonstrate good subject knowledge and generally accurate terminology use, aligning with the upper 3â4 band. Minor factual imprecisions and dated sources prevent progression to excellence. A score of 4 out of 6 reflects this solid but not outstanding performance.

strengths: Research drawn upon is directly relevant and balanced between reliability and unreliability perspectives.
Analysis goes beyond summary to compare methodologies, discuss sample limitations, and evaluate ethical constraints.
Discussion includes thoughtful evaluation of external validity, sample bias, and methodological weaknesses.
Conclusions are generally well-supported by evidence, and counter-evidence is weighed in a reasoned argument.
weaknesses: Corpus of research is limited and heavily weighted toward older studies, reducing breadth and currency.
Lacks quantitative metrics (e.g., error rates, statistical significance) to strengthen analytical depth.
Synthesis across studies could be more systematic, and recommendations or future research directions are missing.
Some broad claims are asserted without sufficient evidence or detailed support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three critical-thinking strands sit comfortably in the 4â6 band, with strong evaluative commentary and supported analysis. Limitations in scope, currency, and systematic synthesis prevent entry into the 7â9 band. Awarding 6 out of 12 reflects an upper-band performance.

strengths: Basic structural conventions (introduction, literature review, discussion, conclusion) are followed, aiding navigation.
Key layout elements (title page, word count, page numbers) are present and correctly placed.
Clear labeling of headings (e.g., âDiscussionâ, âWorks Citedâ) enhances readability.
weaknesses: Inconsistent formatting in the table of contents (indentation, bold styling) and pagination errors.
Mixed citation styles and occasional HTML-style tags detract from academic polish.
Abrupt transitions and uneven section lengths disrupt the flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets the requirements for generally appropriate structure and basic layout, with several lapses in formatting and consistency. This performance aligns with the upper 1â2 band, justifying a score of 2 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific termsâepisodic memory, incidental encoding, BMIS, suggestive interviewing, NICHD protocolâare used accurately and mostly consistently.  Definitions are given for key constructs, though some operational terms (e.g., âreliabilityâ) remain vague.  Minor lapses (confusing âparahippocampalâ with âposterior hippocampusâ functions) keep the work slightly below the fully consistent accuracy expected for the 5-6 band, so a strong 3-4 band award is appropriate.

Basic layout requirementsâtitle page, word count, numbered pages and in-text citationsâare present; however, HTML-style tags, inconsistent bolding, inaccurate TOC page numbers and mixed citation formatting reveal lapses in standard academic presentation.  Consequently, layout considerations are only partially correct, fitting the 1-2 band and meriting the upper score within that range.

The essay demonstrates solid knowledge of developmental neuroscience (hippocampal maturation, parahippocampal gyrus), interview protocols (NICHD) and cognitive factors such as suggestibility.  Sources are generally integrated to support claims, and limitations are acknowledged.  Occasional factual imprecision (e.g., blanket statement that brains finish maturing at 25) and reliance on dated studies prevent it from reaching the âexcellent / consistently relevantâ threshold, situating it in the upper part of the 3-4 band.

A reasonable range of peer-reviewed journal articles and landmark studies is used and briefly justified, showing informed selection; nevertheless, search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria and handling of ethical constraints are not transparently documented.  Source base is narrow (four core empirical papers, many dated) and methodology for evaluating them lacks detail.  Thus the work sits comfortably in the âmostly completeâ 3-4 band rather than demonstrating the breadth and planning required for the top band.

The student opens with a clear contextual hook, states the research question verbatim, and explains why the topic is important in forensic psychology.  Purpose (to determine reliability of childrenâs episodic / autobiographical memory in court) is explicit, and subsequent paragraphs signal how the essay will tackle biological, cognitive and interviewing factors.  Aside from some minor digressions into background narrative, the overall communication of topic, purpose and focus is accurate, concise and maintained throughout.  This aligns with the top-level descriptor for the strand.

Research drawn upon is clearly relevant to the question and covers both unreliability and reliability perspectives.  However, the corpus is limited in size and currency, with heavy weighting toward a handful of older studies, so coverage is âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ relevant.  This merits placement in the 4-6 band; the balanced selection and direct linkage to the argument justify positioning toward its upper end.

The research question is clearly stated and easily identifiable; however, its scope ("to what extent" childrenâs testimonies are reliable) remains quite broad and occasionally drifts into descriptive narrative rather than tightly analytical treatment.  The link between the question and some sections (e.g., historical court practice) is only implicit, signalling partialârather than fullâfocus.  These features fit the 3-4 band where clarity is present but focus is only partially maintained.

The essay follows the conventional EE format with introduction, literature review, discussion and conclusion headings, aiding navigation.  Nevertheless, pagination errors in the table of contents, uneven section lengths and some abrupt transitions indicate only partial adherence to structural conventions.  These features correspond to the âgenerally appropriateâ 1-2 band, warranting the higher score within that band.

The discussion section explicitly evaluates methodological weaknesses (sampling bias, ethics, definitional issues) and weighs counter-evidence before arriving at a reasoned conclusion.  Evaluation is thoughtful but sometimes general, and synthesis across studies could be more systematic.  The argument is clearly structured and partially critical, matching the descriptor for the 4-6 band rather than the fully âcriticalâ 7-9 band.

The student goes beyond summary, comparing methodologies (fMRI vs. field interviews), discussing sample limitations and ethical constraints, and noting how interviewing style mediates memory accuracy.  Most conclusions are supported by cited evidence, though some broad claims (e.g., best-practice steps are âtoo intricateâ to apply) are asserted rather than demonstrated.  This level of analytical depth fits well within the 4-6 band and justifies a mid-to-high score therein.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated but remains broad (âto what extentâ scope); consider narrowing to a specific age group or factor to allow deeper analytical focus.
Inclusion of long-term recall data underscores the complexity of memory reliability in real-life contexts.
The word count is clearly provided on the title page, meeting the IB requirement to state the word limit.
Title is prominently displayed at the start of the report, fulfilling the formatting requirement for report title placement.
The page footer appears blank here; ensure consistent page numbering throughout to meet formatting standards.
The Table of Contents heading follows a consistent style, aiding navigation through the document.
Lack of adult control groups in several studies is highlighted; consider adding comparative data to strengthen conclusions.
Attributing parahippocampal gyrus activation to contextual encoding requires citation; specify source or evidence to support this claim.
Explanation of suggestive interview design in Thompson et al. (1997b) is clear, demonstrating understanding of experimental variables.
Indentation and bold styling in the TOC are inconsistent; apply uniform formatting to improve clarity.
Ensure page numbers in the footer match the TOC entries; missing visible numbering can hinder document navigation.
Background narrative (McMartin case) provides context but veers into detailed description; condense for tighter focus on the research scope.
Effective use of real-world data (âaround 20000 children testifyâ¦ Thompson et al., 1997â) demonstrates relevance and supports the investigationâs significance.
Clear definitions of key brain regions (hippocampus, PFC, PPC) and episodic memory enrich subject understanding and terminology use.
Specifying the academic platforms (e.g., ResearchGate) adds little to source credibility; focus instead on peer-review status and publication details.
The assertion that the brain fully matures at 25 years is overly simplistic; include current developmental neuroscience consensus to refine accuracy.
The literature review describes neural mechanisms but lacks evaluation of fMRI limitations (e.g., sample size, correlational inference); address methodological constraints.
The âDiscussionâ heading clearly signals a shift to critical synthesis, improving logical flow.
The comparison between incidental and intentional encoding studies is useful but would benefit from deeper analysis of potential confounding variables.
Detailed description of Ghetti and Bunge (2012b) methods demonstrates solid integration of empirical research into the essay.
Define suggestibility as a construct before examples to ensure consistent use of key terminology throughout the essay.
Clear chronological description of procedures aids reader comprehension of complex interview protocols.
No quantitative data (e.g., error rates, statistical significance) are presented for Thompson et al.âs findings; including metrics would strengthen the analysis.
The phrase â% accuracyâ is missing a numerical value before the percent sign; ensure statistical figures are precise and complete.
The discussion of control vs. suggestive conditions lacks detail on participant demographics; assess population validity to contextualize results.
Evaluative note on the studyâs external validity and sample limitations demonstrates engagement with research quality.
Section header âArguing that childrenâs testimonies are reliableâ provides clear structure and prepares reader for counterarguments.
Transition to reliability arguments relies on legal history; explicitly link back to the research question to maintain analytical focus.
The detailed protocol description could be streamlined or summarized to maintain focus on its implications for testimony reliability.
Methods description of Erens et al. lacks mention of control group comparability (e.g., training differences); evaluate potential biases.
The use of the BMIS to assess mood demonstrates thoughtful consideration of psychological factors affecting testimony.
Critique of low population validity is insightful; extend by discussing implications for generalizability beyond a single Safe Home.
The age range (4â17) spans developmental stages; discuss how age variability may interact with protocol effects on memory.
Acknowledging that reliability metrics vary across studies reflects an advanced critical understanding of the topic.
Sampling bias critique is valid; could be deepened by suggesting alternative sampling strategies for future studies.
Ethical constraints limiting study content are noted; further discuss how this influences ecological validity of findings.
Nearly all cited evidence predates 2014; integrating more recent research would ensure the essay reflects current developments.
The âWorks Citedâ section is clearly labeled and appropriately placed at document end.
Statement on undetermined correlations of methods and results lacks evidence; propose specific analyses or models to explore these relationships.
Duplicate entries for Erens et al. appear; verify reference list for duplicates and maintain a single correct listing for each source.
Conclusion restates findings but does not propose recommendations or future research directions; consider adding these for completeness.
The âConclusionâ heading is distinct and logically follows the discussion, reinforcing structure.
References are not alphabetized and numbering is inconsistent; apply a consistent citation style (e.g., APA, MLA) throughout.
Web URLs are embedded within references but lack access dates or retrieval information; ensure complete bibliographic details.
